channel.title: Posts on Robert Marshall
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Robert Marshall

item.title: Why I’ve Started Reading the Loeb Classical Library
item.description: Recently, I’ve found myself drawn to the timeless ideas and stories that have shaped human thought. There’s a kind of magic in exploring these ancient texts, and for me, it’s not just about reading summaries or modern interpretations—it’s about engaging directly with the sources themselves. That’s why I’ve started exploring the Loeb Classical Library, a series that brings the works of Ancient Greece and Rome to life in a way that feels both accessible and profound.

item.title: Exploring the Beauty of Persian Poems
item.description: Persian Poems is a collection that feels like stepping into a timeless world of mysticism, beauty, and profound human connection. Spanning over a thousand years, this anthology isn’t just a collection of verses but a reflection on love, spirituality, mortality, and the essence of life itself. Reading it was like being immersed in a lyrical tapestry where every thread holds a story, a lesson, and a moment of transcendence. Published by Everyman’s Library as part of their Pocket Poets series.

item.title: The books I've read in 2024
item.description: These aren’t all the books I’ve read in 2024, but they are the ones that have captured my attention in a meaningful way. From history and philosophy to myth and mystery, each of these works has offered something unique. Whether exploring the revolutionary ideas of Jefferson and Paine in The Apostles of Revolution, delving into the depths of the human psyche with Programmed to Kill, or unraveling the myths and legends in The Poetic Edda and The Prose Edda, these books have sparked my curiosity and expanded my understanding. Other reads like There Is a River: The Story of Edgar Cayce and The Rosicrucian Enlightenment have offered insight into spiritual movements, while Metamorphoses and The Swann Way have taken me on philosophical and literary journeys that continue to resonate. These are the books that have stood out in 2024.

item.title: The Swann Way by Marcel Proust
item.description:
Reading Marcel Proust’s Swann’s Way, the first volume of In Search of Lost Time, was like stepping into a labyrinth of memory, desire, and the passage of time. Proust’s writing is dense, reflective, and unhurried, and it taught me to approach life and literature in a way that values depth over speed.

The Power of Memory

One of the most profound lessons I took from this book was the way memory shapes our experience of the world. Proust’s famous madeleine scene is more than just a poetic exploration of nostalgia; it’s a meditation on how sensory experiences can unlock forgotten emotions and moments.

item.title: Metamorphoses by Ovid
item.description:
Ovid’s Metamorphoses is a sweeping tapestry of myths that explore the transformative power of love, ambition, revenge, and the divine. Spanning creation to Julius Caesar’s apotheosis, it’s not just a collection of myths but a reflection on the fluidity of existence itself. Reading it was like witnessing the world in perpetual motion, where nothing is fixed and change is the only constant.

The Universality of Transformation

The most profound lesson I took from Metamorphoses is the inevitability and universality of change. Every myth in the poem centres on transformation—humans into animals, gods into mortals, and even the cosmos reshaped. Whether it’s Daphne turning into a laurel tree to escape Apollo or Arachne becoming a spider after defying Athena, transformation is portrayed as both a curse and a form of liberation.

item.title: The Rosicrucian Enlightenment by Frances A. Yates
item.description:
Frances A. Yates’ The Rosicrucian Enlightenment opened my eyes to a fascinating but often overlooked chapter of history where mysticism, science, and politics converged. The book is not just a historical study; it’s an exploration of how ideas, even esoteric ones, can shape the course of history.

The Power of Esoteric Ideas

One of the first things I learned was how the Rosicrucian manifestos of the early 17th century—anonymous texts promising a new era of knowledge, enlightenment, and harmony—captured the imagination of an age. These texts weren’t just idle fantasies; they were a response to the intellectual and spiritual ferment of the Renaissance, combining Hermeticism, alchemy, and reformist Christianity with the burgeoning scientific revolution.

item.title: There Is a River: The Story of Edgar Cayce by Joseph Sugrue
item.description: There Is a River: The Story of Edgar Cayce by Joseph Sugrue is a fascinating and comprehensive biography of one of the most intriguing figures in the fields of psychic phenomena and holistic healing: Edgar Cayce. Through this book, I gained a deeper understanding of Cayce’s life, his extraordinary abilities, and the profound impact his work had on both the spiritual and medical communities. What I learned from Sugrue’s account is not only about the events of Cayce’s life but also about the power of human potential and the mystery of the mind.

item.title: The Poetic Edda & The Prose Edda, by Sæmundr Sigfússon, Snorri Sturluson
item.description:
Reading The Poetic Edda and The Prose Edda was like opening a gateway to the mythological heart of Norse culture. These works, brimming with gods, giants, and epic tales of creation and destruction, taught me not only about the myths themselves but also about the worldview and values of the societies that created them.

The Interconnectedness of Myth and Culture

One of the most striking lessons was how deeply Norse mythology is intertwined with the natural world and the human experience. Tales of Yggdrasil, the World Tree, and the Nine Realms reflect an interconnected universe where every action reverberates across creation. These myths convey a profound respect for nature’s cycles of life, death, and rebirth, which mirrored the harsh realities of living in the unforgiving Scandinavian landscape.

item.title: Programmed to Kill: The Politics of Serial Murder by David McGowan
item.description:
Programmed to Kill was an unsettling yet eye-opening experience that forced me to reconsider everything I thought I knew about serial killers and the broader societal forces at play. David McGowan’s core argument—that many infamous serial murders might not be the work of lone, psychopathic individuals but instead tied to larger, organised conspiracies—left me grappling with some profoundly uncomfortable questions.

The Myth of the “Lone Wolf”

One of the most striking revelations from the book is how the archetype of the solitary, deranged killer is rarely questioned. McGowan explores how media and law enforcement reinforce this image, even when evidence suggests something far more complex. For instance, he highlights bizarre inconsistencies in high-profile cases, such as unusual access to victims, suspicious ties to military or intelligence organisations, and unexplained lapses in police investigations.

item.title: John Ferling's The Apostles of Revolution
item.description: John Ferling’s The Apostles of Revolution is a masterfully written exploration of how the lives and ideas of Thomas Jefferson, Thomas Paine, and James Monroe helped shape not only the American Revolution but also the broader struggle for liberty across Europe. Reading it gave me a deeper understanding of the revolutionary era—not just as a series of political events, but as a moment of profound ideological transformation with global consequences.
